#### Learning 99 ‚Äî RECORDING an adopted decision is not the same as STAMPING it ‚Äúadopted‚Äù: when the owner picks a recommendation that has open sub-options, resolving a sub-option can invalidate a top-level claim the source doc made under a *different* sub-choice ‚Äî so propagate the chosen sub-option‚Äôs consequences and CORRECT the now-stale claims, don‚Äôt just flip a Status line. (S105, adopt Hybrid documentation strategy ‚Äî `docs/planning/quarto-documentation-future-proofing-analysis.md`)

**The trap.** S104‚Äôs analysis recommended Hybrid (Option B) and, in ¬ß8,
stated ‚Äúonly Option A would intersect the CRAN submission; under B/C the
plan is unaffected‚Äù ‚Äî TRUE *only if* the ¬ß6.3 manual sub-decision stayed
at (a) keep-as-knitr-vignette. When the owner adopted B **with ¬ß6.3 ‚Üí
(b)** (reposition the manual onto the website, dropping it from the CRAN
vignette set), that removes a CRAN vignette = changes the package
contents `R CMD check` sees, so the adopted path now DOES intersect the
resubmission. Flipping the Status to ‚ÄúADOPTED‚Äù without fixing ¬ß8 would
have left a self-contradicting policy doc ‚Äî the kind of stale
load-bearing claim FM \#11/#20 warn about, here introduced by the act of
recording rather than by memory drift.

**What ‚Äúrecord the decision‚Äù actually entails (the checklist that
worked):** (1) flip the doc‚Äôs own decision surfaces ‚Äî Status header,
TL;DR, the ¬ß7 options-table verdict, the ‚Äúdefault if unaddressed‚Äù line ‚Äî
so it reads as policy, not a pending recommendation; (2) RESOLVE every
open sub-decision the chosen option carried (here ¬ß6.3 ‚Üí (b)) and mark
it inline where the option is described; (3) re-derive and CORRECT any
consequence the source doc asserted conditional on a *different*
sub-choice (¬ß8‚Äôs ‚Äúonly A intersects‚Äù); (4) record the implementation
slices where future sessions look ‚Äî for this project, the analysis doc
(a new ¬ß7.1 slices table with per-slice CRAN-risk + ordering) + ROADMAP
‚ÄúPlanned‚Äù; (5) CHANGELOG entry (a documentation-process decision =
dev-process history ‚Üí CHANGELOG only, no NEWS ‚Äî
\[\[backlog-vs-changelog-placement\]\]); (6) NO auto-memory ‚Äî the
decision now lives in the repo, so a memory would duplicate the source
of truth (\[\[consult-project-source-of-truth\]\]).

**The deliverable boundary held (FM \#18).** ‚ÄúAdopt Hybrid‚Äù is a
DECISION-recording session; it converts NOTHING. The first conversion
slice is a separate, owner-approved session. The owner‚Äôs own menu
framing (‚Äú(A) Decide the recommendation ‚Ä¶ the first implementable slice
is a separate session‚Äù) made this explicit, and recording the slices as
a ¬ß7.1 table (with the slice-4 resubmission-coordination gate) sets up
those sessions without starting them. The one CRAN-touching slice (the
manual) is flagged as gated on the CRAN plan, not free-standing. **Two
`AskUserQuestion`s up front** pinned the two genuine owner sub-decisions
(¬ß6.3 manual disposition; file-issues-or-not) *before* writing ‚Äî they
changed exactly what got recorded. **Right-sized SOLO under ultracode:**
editing 4‚Äì5 docs to record a decision the owner just made ‚Äî facts
already established and already adversarially verified in S104 ‚Äî has no
breadth to fan out and no fresh claims to verify; a Workflow would be
theater (the S101/S102/S103/S104 call). Verification = a firsthand
cross-document consistency re-read (does ¬ß6.3 = ¬ß7 = ¬ß7.1 = ¬ß8 = ROADMAP
= CHANGELOG tell ONE story?). **Reflexes:** \[recording an adopted
decision ‚âÝ stamping ‚Äúadopted‚Äù ‚Äî resolve open sub-options and CORRECT any
source claim that assumed a different sub-choice\]\[flip ALL the doc‚Äôs
decision surfaces: Status, TL;DR, options-table verdict, default
line\]\[record slices where future sessions look: analysis doc +
ROADMAP + CHANGELOG entry, no NEWS\]\[no auto-memory for a decision now
recorded in the repo\]\[decision-recording converts nothing ‚Äî FM \#18;
slices are separate owner-approved sessions\]\[gate the one
CRAN-touching slice on the CRAN plan, keep the zero-risk slices
independent\]\[ask the genuine owner sub-decisions up front via
AskUserQuestion\]\[verify a decision-recording deliverable by
cross-document consistency re-read,
SOLO\]\[right-size-SOLO-even-under-ultracode\]. **Apply:** when the
owner says ‚Äúadopt ‚Äù ‚Äî treat it as a decision-recording deliverable
(convert nothing); ask the option‚Äôs open sub-decisions first; then flip
every decision surface in the source doc, resolve the sub-decisions
inline, re-derive and fix any consequence the doc stated under a
different sub-choice, record the implementation slices in the doc +
ROADMAP + CHANGELOG (no NEWS, no auto-memory), and verify by reading all
the touched docs as one consistent story.

#### Learning 100 ‚Äî Converting a doc Rmd ‚Üí Quarto `.qmd` is not just a rename: (a) the target format has SEMANTIC differences the source didn‚Äôt (Quarto treats `:::` as fenced-div/callout syntax; R Markdown doesn‚Äôt), so you must RENDER to catch them ‚Äî a static rename hides them; (b) verify the conversion changes no *package* contents by applying the `.Rbuildignore` regexes to BOTH filenames, don‚Äôt assume ‚Äúit was ignored so the new one is too‚Äù; (c) when the doc isn‚Äôt reproducibly renderable (dead hardcoded paths, unmaterialized packages), render with `--no-execute` to verify FORMAT validity and STATE the execution limitation ‚Äî do not fake a full render, and do NOT ‚Äúfix‚Äù the doc into being executable (that‚Äôs a behavior change, not a format conversion); (d) preserve the author‚Äôs historical prose byte-for-byte rather than rewrite it to silence a cosmetic warning whose output is verified correct (FM \#22). (S106, Quarto Hybrid ¬ß7.1 slice 1 ‚Äî `inst/extdata/meeting_notes.Rmd` ‚Üí `.qmd`)

**What happened.** Slice 1 of the adopted Hybrid doc policy: convert the
build-ignored developer doc `inst/extdata/meeting_notes.Rmd` to `.qmd`.
The mechanical change is tiny ‚Äî `git mv` + one YAML line
(`output: html_document` ‚Üí `format: html`, matching the two
already-`.qmd` sibling dev docs); `git diff -M` reported
`similarity index 99%`, body byte-for-byte. The *value* was in the
verification, which surfaced three things a rename-only ‚Äúconversion‚Äù
would have missed: - **A real Rmd‚Üíqmd semantic difference.** Rendering
the `.qmd` (Quarto 1.7.33) emitted a warning that the string
`nprcgenekeepr:::` ‚Äúlooked like a fenced div.‚Äù Quarto treats `:::` as
fenced-div / callout syntax; R Markdown does not. The token was R‚Äôs
internal-function operator quoted from a 2020 CRAN-review reply, sitting
in prose. Checking the rendered HTML proved it rendered correctly
(mid-line `:::` is not a div delimiter ‚Äî a heuristic false-positive), so
it was left byte-faithful. But the general point holds: **a static
rename would never have revealed it; only the render did.** Other latent
Quarto-vs-Rmd differences (header attributes
[`{}`](https://rdrr.io/r/base/Paren.html), raw-HTML/`$math$` handling,
callouts) live in the same blind spot. - **The doc isn‚Äôt reproducibly
renderable ‚Äî and that‚Äôs not mine to ‚Äúfix.‚Äù** Five embedded R chunks
hardcode 2020-era absolute paths
(`/Users/rmsharp/.../20160816_GeneticManagementTools`) and need packages
absent from the default library (the standing renv-not-materialized
condition). `include=FALSE` hides output but the chunk still *executes*,
so a full render fails on the dead paths even with packages. This is a
historical meeting-notes log, not a live computational document. The
build-equivalent (SAFEGUARDS ‚ÄúVerify the Build Equivalent‚Äù) was
therefore satisfied by `quarto render --no-execute` (verifies YAML +
markdown + structure ‚Üí 81 KB HTML, code shown not run) with the
execution limitation **stated, not silently skipped**. Making it
re-executable (e.g.¬Ý`eval: false` globally, or repointing the paths)
would have been a behavior/content change masquerading as a format
conversion ‚Äî FM \#8 / the SAFEGUARDS two-mode ‚Äúwhile I‚Äôm at it‚Äù trap. -
**‚ÄúIt was build-ignored‚Äù is not proof the new file is.** I confirmed it
by reading both relevant `.Rbuildignore` regexes
(`^inst/extdata/meeting_notes\.` ‚Äî extension-agnostic ‚Äî and
`^inst/extdata/.*\.qmd$`) AND by running every `.Rbuildignore` pattern
against both `meeting_notes.Rmd` and `meeting_notes.qmd` in R
(`ships=FALSE` for both). Had the ignore pattern been extension-specific
(`...\.Rmd$`), the conversion would have started *shipping* the new
`.qmd` into the CRAN tarball ‚Äî a silent package-contents change. For an
archived package mid-resubmission (Learning 95), that‚Äôs exactly the kind
of invisible regression to rule out, not assume.

**Reflexes:** \[Rmd‚Üíqmd is a format change with SEMANTIC differences
(`:::` fenced divs), not just a rename ‚Äî RENDER to catch them\]\[verify
a doc conversion changes no package contents by running the
`.Rbuildignore` regexes against BOTH old and new filenames, not by
assuming\]\[when a doc isn‚Äôt reproducibly renderable (dead paths /
unmaterialized packages), render `--no-execute` to verify FORMAT and
STATE the execution limitation ‚Äî never fake a full render\]\[do NOT
‚Äúfix‚Äù a historical doc into being executable during a format conversion
‚Äî that‚Äôs a behavior change (FM \#8)\]\[preserve the author‚Äôs historical
prose byte-faithful; don‚Äôt rewrite it to silence a cosmetic warning
whose output is verified correct (FM \#22)\]\[`git mv` for doc renames ‚Äî
preserves history, fully reversible\]\[1-and-done: one slice, do not
bundle the next (FM \#18/#25)\]\[right-size SOLO for a single-file
mechanical conversion even under ultracode\]. **Apply:** when converting
any doc between markup engines ‚Äî do the minimal faithful change
(`git mv` + the format-line), then RENDER (using `--no-execute` if the
doc isn‚Äôt reproducibly executable) to catch target-format semantic
differences, confirm via the ignore/build rules that no shipped-contents
changed, state any verification you could not perform, and leave the
author‚Äôs content byte-faithful unless a real output defect (not a
cosmetic warning) forces a change.

#### Learning 101 ‚Äî A ‚Äúmake it render/build‚Äù doc deliverable is verified by building it through the REAL consumer‚Äôs integration path, not a proxy renderer ‚Äî and an adversarial reviewer‚Äôs ‚Äúcorrection‚Äù is a claim to check against ground truth, not a verdict to act on. (S107, Quarto Hybrid ¬ß7.1 slice 2 ‚Äî pkgdown mixed mode + `vignettes/articles/breeding-group-formation.qmd`)

**What happened.** Slice 2 of the adopted Hybrid doc policy: stand up
pkgdown mixed `.qmd`/`.Rmd` mode and author the first Quarto pkgdown
article (a scripted breeding-group-formation walkthrough on shipped
`examplePedigree` data). Three things made the verification the real
work: - **Recon against the canonical source corrected an imprecise plan
instruction.** The ¬ß7.1 policy note said ‚Äúadd a `_quarto.yml`
(`project: render: ['*.qmd']`)‚Äù without saying *where*. The
authoritative pkgdown docs (+ usethis `use_article()`) say the
`_quarto.yml` and the `.qmd` both live **inside `vignettes/articles/`**
(pkgdown turns that dir into a Quarto project), and a single
`.Rbuildignore` line `^vignettes/articles$` then makes the whole dir
website-only ‚Äî covering the article, the `_quarto.yml`, and any
`.quarto/` cache. A root `_quarto.yml` (my Phase-1 assumption) would
have needed its own ignore entry and could have pulled the `.Rmd`
vignettes into a Quarto project. Reading the source of truth beat acting
on the plausible-sounding policy paraphrase. - **Render-only is a proxy;
build through the real consumer.** `quarto render` proves the article‚Äôs
R chunks execute (they did, ~1.7 s, deterministic via `set_seed(1L)`).
But the deliverable‚Äôs actual claim is ‚Äú**pkgdown** mixed mode works.‚Äù So
I installed pkgdown 2.2.0 + the `quarto` R package locally and ran
[`pkgdown::build_article`](https://pkgdown.r-lib.org/reference/build_articles.html)
‚Äî which surfaced an integration detail a renderer never would: pkgdown‚Äôs
name for the article is `articles/breeding-group-formation` (with the
`articles/` prefix), and the build only succeeded once I used it (the
bare stem errored ‚ÄúCan‚Äôt find article‚Äù). The two-path verification
(quarto render AND pkgdown build) is what makes ‚Äúmixed mode is stood up‚Äù
a tested claim rather than a hopeful one. Zero CRAN risk was likewise
*proven* (a real `R CMD build` tarball shows the `vignettes/articles/`
tree absent and the shipping vignettes unaffected), not assumed. -
**Verify the verifier.** A fresh adversarial reviewer flagged the
article‚Äôs ‚Äúthreshold 0.015625 = 1/64 = second cousins‚Äù as wrong,
asserting 1/64 is *third* cousins. Checked before acting: it confused
the **coefficient of relationship** (r; second cousins = 1/32) with the
**kinship coefficient** (œÜ = r/2; second cousins = 1/64) ‚Äî and this
package computes œÜ. The package‚Äôs own manual
(`vignettes/manual_components/_breeding_group_formation.Rmd`) says the
default ignores ‚Äúrelatedness more distant than second cousins.‚Äù The
original was correct; I left it unchanged. An adversarial pass is only
as trustworthy as the check you run on *its* claims.

**Reflexes:** \[verify a doc/render deliverable through the REAL
consumer‚Äôs build path (here
[`pkgdown::build_article`](https://pkgdown.r-lib.org/reference/build_articles.html)),
not just a standalone renderer ‚Äî the integration path catches
discovery/naming/wiring the renderer can‚Äôt\]\[read the
canonical/authoritative source for config placement; don‚Äôt act on a
plan‚Äôs paraphrase of it\]\[prove zero-CRAN-risk for a website artifact
with a real `R CMD build` tarball + build-ignore the whole
`vignettes/articles/` dir ‚Äî don‚Äôt assume\]\[an adversarial reviewer‚Äôs
‚Äúcorrection‚Äù is a candidate, not a verdict ‚Äî check it against ground
truth (the math AND the package‚Äôs own docs) before changing anything;
distinguish kinship coefficient œÜ from coefficient of relationship
r\]\[build a how-to article on the function‚Äôs own roxygen `@examples`
pipeline so it exercises the same code `R CMD check` runs\]\[seed once
up front (`set_seed`) for reproducible rendered stochastic
output\]\[right-size: recon as a Workflow for breadth/context-economy,
author the prose SOLO, verify through real build paths\]. **Apply:**
when a deliverable is ‚Äúmake X render/build on surface Y,‚Äù verify by
building it on Y itself (install Y‚Äôs real toolchain if needed), prove
any ‚Äúwon‚Äôt ship / no risk‚Äù claim with the actual build artifact, ground
config choices in the authoritative docs rather than a plan‚Äôs
restatement, and treat every adversarial finding ‚Äî including a
reviewer‚Äôs ‚Äî as a claim to verify, not accept.

#### Learning 102 ‚Äî When documenting a function‚Äôs outputs/scoring, the GROUND TRUTH is the implementation run on real data plus the scoring source ‚Äî NOT the function‚Äôs own roxygen, which can drift. Run the pipeline first; read the ordering/scoring code; treat `@return`/`@param` prose as a hint. (S108, Quarto Hybrid ¬ß7.1 ‚Äî second article `vignettes/articles/genetic-value-analysis.qmd`, a scripted `reportGV()` walkthrough)

**What happened.** Authoring an accurate how-to for
[`reportGV()`](https://github.com/rmsharp/nprcgenekeepr/reference/reportGV.md)
meant getting three things right that the package‚Äôs OWN roxygen states
incorrectly ‚Äî caught only by reading the implementation: (1)
`orderReport.R` `@return` says the High-Value mean-kinship tier is ‚Äúmean
kinship less than 0.25,‚Äù but the code (`orderReport.R:63`) gates on the
**z-score** (`zScores <= 0.25`), not raw mean kinship; (2) `calcFEFG.R`
roxygen calls the founder weight `p` the ‚Äúaverage number of
descendants,‚Äù but the code computes Mendelian-halved proportional
**contributions** (`calcFounderContributions.R`); (3) `reportGV.R`
`@return` says it returns ‚ÄúA dataframe,‚Äù but it returns a **list** of
class `nprcgenekeeprGV`. The article is correct in all three because it
was written from the code + a real run, not from the roxygen.

**Run-the-pipeline-first paid off.** Executing the GVA on shipped
`examplePedigree` (deterministic via `set_seed(1L)` ‚Äî the gene-drop
behind genome uniqueness / `fe` / `fg` is stochastic; mean kinship is
not) produced the real numbers the prose describes (327-animal
population of interest, 199/128 High/Low Value, `fe` 109.67 / `fg`
47.62) and surfaced the teaching example for free: the rank-1 animal has
higher genome uniqueness but NOT the lowest mean kinship ‚Äî which is
exactly why the ranking uses both metrics. The article prints computed
values in chunks rather than hardcoding them, so the rendered output is
the ground truth.

**Verify-the-verifier held again (Learning 101).** This time the
adversarial reviewer‚Äôs flags were ALL real ‚Äî I confirmed each against
the code before applying three precision fixes: `meanKinship.R` is
`colMeans` over the whole matrix incl.¬Ýthe diagonal ‚Üí ‚Äúall animals
incl.¬Ýitself,‚Äù not ‚Äúevery other‚Äù; `orderReport.R:59` tier-2 sort breaks
ties on ascending z-score; `orderReport.R:32` imports tier also requires
`id %in% founders`. An adversarial pass that returns ‚Äúno must-fix‚Äù still
earns its cost: it converted three latent imprecisions into fixes and
independently confirmed the article out-corrects the roxygen.

**Discovered, not mine (flagged, not fixed ‚Äî FM \#8).** The sibling S107
article `breeding-group-formation.qmd` comments its focal set is ‚Äúthe
founders still in the colony,‚Äù but the filter
`!(is.na(sire) & is.na(dam))` selects **non-founders** (‚â• 1 known
parent). A one-line prose inversion; left for an owner-approved session.

**Reflexes:** \[run the function‚Äôs real pipeline on shipped data and let
the printed output be the ground truth before writing a descriptive
sentence\]\[read the scoring/ordering implementation
(`orderReport`/`rankSubjects`/`calcFEFG`), not the function‚Äôs `@return`,
when the doc‚Äôs whole job is to explain behavior ‚Äî roxygen drifts\]\[seed
once up front for reproducible stochastic rendered output\]\[an
adversarial reviewer‚Äôs flags are claims to verify against code, even
when they all turn out right\]\[a how-to built on the function‚Äôs own
`@examples` shape exercises the code `R CMD check` runs\]\[right-size:
recon Workflow for breadth, author SOLO, verify through real build
paths\]. **Apply:** documenting any function‚Äôs outputs/scoring ‚Äî derive
every claim from a real run + the implementation; where the roxygen is
wrong, FIX-FORWARD in the doc and note the roxygen for a future repair
session; flag, don‚Äôt fix, errors you find in sibling artifacts.

#### Learning 103 ‚Äî Ground-truth-first documentation also means running the UNHAPPY paths: a function‚Äôs error/edge behavior is part of what you are documenting, and running it can refute an assumption you would otherwise have written down as fact. (S109, Quarto Hybrid ¬ß7.1 ‚Äî third article `vignettes/articles/studbook-quality-control.qmd`, a scripted `qcStudbook()` walkthrough)

**What happened.** Documenting
[`qcStudbook()`](https://github.com/rmsharp/nprcgenekeepr/reference/qcStudbook.md)
meant describing two modes (`reportErrors = FALSE` production vs `TRUE`
diagnostic) and how each handles every category of bad input. From
reading the code I had *assumed* invalid dates were silently coerced to
`NA` in production mode. Running `qcStudbook(pedInvalidDates)` showed it
actually **stops** (‚Äúinvalid dates on row(s) 3 and 4‚Äù). Running each
shipped error-demo set in both modes produced the exact, verified
behavior table the article rests on ‚Äî auto-corrected
(female-sire/male-dam, exact duplicates), fatal (missing column, invalid
date, sire==dam, young parent, period-in-ID), and the diagnostic list‚Äôs
named elements (returning `NULL` when clean). The shipped
`pedGood`/`pedFemaleSireMaleDam`/`pedInvalidDates`/`pedDuplicateIds`/`pedMissingBirth`/`pedSameMaleIsSireAndDam`
data sets are purpose-built worked examples ‚Äî find and use them rather
than constructing inputs.

**Determinism by reading, not hoping.**
[`qcStudbook()`](https://github.com/rmsharp/nprcgenekeepr/reference/qcStudbook.md)
has no random component (no seed needed ‚Äî unlike the GVA/breeding
siblings whose `reportGV`/`groupAddAssign` gene-drop is stochastic), and
its one [`Sys.Date()`](https://rdrr.io/r/base/Sys.time.html) dependency
(`calcAge` for living animals) is bypassed because `examplePedigree`
ships an `age` column, so no live-date value enters the displayed
output. Confirmed empirically by the render-determinism review lens: two
render passes produced byte-identical HTML. When a fatal call must
appear in a render, wrap it in
`tryCatch(..., error = function(e) cat(conditionMessage(e)))` so the
real message is shown without breaking the build (vs an `#| error: true`
chunk whose toolchain support I had not proven here).

**Two-lens adversarial review + verify-the-verifier (Learning
101/102).** Ran the review as two parallel lenses (code-correctness vs
the implementation; pedagogy + render-determinism). No must-fix; I
confirmed each flag against the code before acting ‚Äî the `H` sex-code
flag was real (`qcStudbook` calls `convertSexCodes(ignoreHerm = TRUE)` ‚Üí
`H`/`HERMAPHRODITE`/`4` fold to `U`, never output), so I fixed the
article (out-correcting an over-broad ‚ÄúM/F/U/H‚Äù claim) and added a
`## Setup` section for house-style parallelism with the siblings; left
the ‚Äúdefensible digest‚Äù column-rename nit as-is.

**Reflexes:** \[run the unhappy paths, not just the happy path ‚Äî
error/edge behavior is part of the documentation surface, and running it
can refute a code-reading assumption\]\[prefer shipped purpose-built
example data sets over constructed inputs\]\[prove render determinism
both by reading for RNG +
[`Sys.Date()`](https://rdrr.io/r/base/Sys.time.html) reachability AND by
a byte-identical double render\]\[show a fatal call via `tryCatch` +
`cat(conditionMessage(e))` so the build stays clean\]\[two adversarial
lenses (correctness vs pedagogy/render) beat one for a doc article;
still verify every flag against code\]. **Apply:** documenting any
function with modes/error handling ‚Äî exercise every mode on real data
and let the output be the table; a behavior you only read in the source
is a hypothesis until you run it.

#### Learning 104 ‚Äî When the documentation IS a data visualization, the figure‚Äôs input filter is part of the truth: read what the plot actually includes/excludes, and run it on the example data ‚Äî a plot built from incomplete data can show the OPPOSITE of reality, not just an attenuated version. (S110, Quarto Hybrid ¬ß7.1 ‚Äî fourth article `vignettes/articles/age-sex-pyramid.qmd`, a scripted `getPyramidPlot()` walkthrough)

**What happened.** `getPyramidPlot(qcPed)` looked like it would plot
‚Äúthe living colony,‚Äù and a naive article would have said so. Running it
(and `fillBins`) showed the pyramid plots only living animals **with a
known age** ‚Äî 46 of 89 living, because 43 living animals lack a birth
date and so cannot be aged. Those 43 are *all male*, so the plot shows
35 F / 11 M (‚âà 3:1 female) while the living colony is actually 35 F / 54
M (male-majority). The visualization shows the **reverse** of the real
sex ratio. The article was built on the verified numbers and turned this
into its central lesson (missing birth dates can *invert* a demographic
plot, not just shrink it ‚Äî quality-control first), which the second
adversarial lens then sharpened from ‚Äúunder-counts males‚Äù to ‚Äúthe skew
is reversed.‚Äù

**Determinism for a base-graphics figure.** The pyramid is
[`plotrix::pyramid.plot()`](https://plotrix.github.io/plotrix/reference/pyramid.plot.html)
(base graphics), so the plotting call must be the chunk‚Äôs last
expression, and `#| results: hide` keeps the function‚Äôs (non-plot)
return value out of the rendered output. The render is deterministic in
*shape* ‚Äî no RNG, no seed (unlike the GVA/breeding siblings), and
`qcPed` ships a frozen `age` column ‚Äî but the title embeds
[`lubridate::now()`](https://lubridate.tidyverse.org/reference/now.html),
so the date label is the build date. I characterized that honestly
rather than trying to force byte-identical output.

**Two-lens review + verify-the-verifier (Learning 101/102/103).**
Code-correctness re-ran every number against `qcPed` (all held). The
pedagogy lens caught the reversed-skew understatement and the V&R
reference over-attribution; I confirmed both against the data (living
35F/54M) and the paper‚Äôs actual subject before applying. Found (not
fixed, FM \#8) a `getPyramidPlot` `@return` roxygen drift (claims
`par('mar')`; returns `pyramid.plot()`‚Äôs value) ‚Äî flagged for the
roxygen-repair session.

**Reflexes:** \[for documentation-of-a-plot, read the plot‚Äôs input
filter (`fillBins`: living + non-NA age) and run it on the example data
‚Äî the figure‚Äôs coverage is part of the claim\]\[a plot from incomplete
data can show the OPPOSITE of reality, not just an attenuated version ‚Äî
check stratum-by-stratum (here the dropped 43 are all male, flipping the
sex ratio)\]\[base-graphics chunk: plot is the last expression +
`#| results: hide` to drop the return value\]\[a `now()` title is an
honest ‚Äúas of render date,‚Äù not a determinism defect to hide\].
**Apply:** documenting any plotting function ‚Äî derive the description
from what the function actually plots on real data, and stress-test
whether missing data could invert (not merely shrink) the visual
conclusion.

#### Learning 105 ‚Äî A flagged error from a previous session is a claim to re-verify, not a fact to action: confirm it two ways (internal corroboration + ground truth on the example data) before correcting, and right-size a one-line fix as SOLO. (S111, doc fix `vignettes/articles/breeding-group-formation.qmd` ‚Äî corrected the inverted ‚Äúfounders‚Äù ‚Üí ‚Äúnon-founders‚Äù focal-population description)

**What happened.** S108 discovered, and S109/S110 carried forward, that
the breeding-group article called its focal set ‚Äúthe founders still in
the colony‚Äù while the filter `!(is.na(sire) & is.na(dam)) & is.na(exit)`
actually selects **non-founders**. Rather than trust the prior-session
flag and swap the word, I confirmed it two independent ways: (1)
**internal corroboration** ‚Äî the same sentence trims the pedigree to the
focal set ‚Äúplus the ancestors needed to compute their kinships,‚Äù and
founders by definition have no ancestors, so the focal set logically
*must* be non-founders; (2) **ground truth** ‚Äî ran
`qcStudbook(examplePedigree)` + the article‚Äôs own filter: 327 focal
animals, none founders, all with ‚â• 1 known parent (1,668 founders in the
studbook, zero in the focal set). Both agreed with the flag, so the fix
was safe. The correction also adds an inline definition (‚Äúthose with at
least one known parent‚Äù) so the term is clear without the reader
cross-referencing the code.

**Right-sizing under ultracode.** A one-line prose correction with a
deterministic two-way verification has no breadth to fan out and no
competing claims to adjudicate ‚Äî it was done SOLO. Spawning a Workflow
would have been theater (the same S100/S106 call for genuinely
single-threaded mechanical work). Ultracode means *exhaustive and
correct*, not *always multi-agent*; here exhaustiveness was the two-way
verification, not parallelism.

**Reflexes:** \[a flagged error inherited from a predecessor is a
hypothesis to verify against code + data, not a fact to action blindly ‚Äî
even when the predecessor was reliable\]\[confirm a prose-vs-logic
mismatch two ways: internal corroboration (does the surrounding text
agree?) + ground truth (run the actual filter on the example
data)\]\[when correcting an inverted term, add an inline definition so
the fix also removes the ambiguity that allowed the
inversion\]\[right-size: a one-line fix with deterministic verification
is SOLO, not a Workflow\]. **Apply:** actioning any ‚Äúdiscovered, not
mine‚Äù flag from a prior session ‚Äî re-verify it firsthand before editing;
the discoverer couldn‚Äôt fix it precisely because it wasn‚Äôt their
deliverable, so the claim has never itself been re-checked.

#### Learning 106 ‚Äî Regenerating a generated artifact (here `man/` via `roxygenise()`) with a dev tool newer than the committed baseline silently reformats EVERY file and migrates config ‚Äî read `git diff --stat` after any codegen step, and if it touches files beyond your edits, revert the version migration and apply the change surgically rather than bundling a tooling bump into a content fix. And: re-verifying inherited flags can REFUTE them, not just confirm them. (S112, roxygen-repair pass ‚Äî fixed 3 of 4 inherited `@return`/`p` drifts across `orderReport`/`calcFEFG`/`reportGV` + the `calcFE`/`calcFG` siblings)

**What happened (the blast-radius trap).** The deliverable was a 5-line
roxygen prose fix + a `man/` regen. Running
[`roxygen2::roxygenise()`](https://roxygen2.r-lib.org/reference/roxygenize.html)
rewrote ~30 `.Rd` files and changed `DESCRIPTION` ‚Äî far beyond the 4
functions I edited. Cause: the committed `man/` was generated with
roxygen2 **7.3.2** (`RoxygenNote: 7.3.2`), but the dev library has
**8.0.0**, which reformats every page (data-doc `\usage` `qcPed` ‚Üí
`data(qcPed)`, re-wrapped `\value`) and migrates the field
(`RoxygenNote:` ‚Üí `Config/roxygen2/version:`). Committing it would have
bundled a tooling-baseline migration ‚Äî a deliberate, CRAN-coordinated
decision ‚Äî into a doc fix. Countermeasure:
`git checkout -- man/ DESCRIPTION` to drop the version artifacts, then
hand-edited the four affected `\value` blocks to match source, keeping
the diff to exactly 5 `R/` + 4 `man/` files. The 7.3.2‚Üí8.0.0 migration
was flagged as a separate task. **General rule: after any
codegen/regeneration step, read `git diff --stat`; if it touches files
you did not intend, a version/tooling mismatch is reformatting them ‚Äî do
not commit it as part of a content change.** (This is the codegen
sibling of SAFEGUARDS ‚ÄúVerify Render-Dependency Completeness‚Äù ‚Äî a tool‚Äôs
*version* is a render dependency.)

**Re-verifying inherited flags can refute, not just confirm (extends
Learning 105).** Four `@return`/param drifts were inherited as code-read
claims (three from S108, one from S110) never confirmed against runtime.
An 8-agent verification workflow (one verifier + one adversarial
cross-checker per claim) confirmed three and **refuted the fourth**:
`getPyramidPlot.R`‚Äôs `@return` (‚Äúthe return value of `par('mar')`‚Äù) is
correct ‚Äî the function returns
[`plotrix::pyramid.plot()`](https://plotrix.github.io/plotrix/reference/pyramid.plot.html)‚Äôs
value, but `pyramid.plot` ends with `return(oldmar)` where
`oldmar <- par("mar")`, so the returned value *is* a `par("mar")` vector
(verified: returns `c(5.1, 4.1, 4.1, 2.1)`). S110‚Äôs flag was a
false-dichotomy code-read (‚Äúreturns pyramid.plot, not par(‚Äòmar‚Äô)‚Äù) that
never ran the function. Trusting it would have replaced a correct
`@return` with a wrong one. The cross-check also surfaced same-defect
instances beyond the named scope (the `p` description in sibling
`calcFE`/`calcFG`; a second `orderReport` `@return` bullet), which the
owner approved folding in.

**Right-sizing: this one earned a Workflow where S111 did not.**
Adjudicating four independent inherited claims that ship to CRAN ‚Äî each
needing a code trace + an adversarial check ‚Äî has real breadth and
competing claims, so the verify+refute fan-out was warranted (contrast
Learning 105‚Äôs single deterministic one-liner, correctly SOLO).
Verification fans out; the mutation (edits + regen) stays
single-threaded in the main loop to keep control and avoid worktree
conflicts.

**Scope discipline held with one disclosed extension.** Fixed exactly
the agreed `p`-description in `calcFE`/`calcFG` (kept their `r` clause),
and flagged ‚Äî did not fix ‚Äî adjacent pre-existing defects (`calcFE`‚Äôs
`@return` mentions `r` though `FE = 1/sum(p^2)` has none; the
`sum( (p^2) / r}` formula has unbalanced parens; the pre-existing
`reportGV.Rd` `\title`-ends-in-period `checkRd` note).

**Reflexes:** \[after ANY codegen/regeneration step, read
`git diff --stat` before trusting it ‚Äî a diff touching files beyond your
edits means a tool/version mismatch is reformatting them; revert and
apply surgically\]\[never bundle a generated-tooling version migration
(here roxygen 7.3.2‚Üí8.0.0) into a content fix ‚Äî it‚Äôs a separate,
deliberate decision\]\[a generated artifact (`man/*.Rd`) may be
hand-edited as a scoped, disclosed exception when regenerating would
force a version migration ‚Äî the content matches source, so a later
proper regen reconciles cleanly\]\[re-verify inherited flags against
runtime, not just code-read ‚Äî they can be WRONG, not merely unconfirmed;
one of four here was\]\[verification fans out (verifier + adversarial
refuter per claim), mutation stays single-threaded\]\[doc-change
build-equivalent =
[`tools::checkRd()`](https://rdrr.io/r/tools/checkRd.html) +
[`pkgload::load_all()`](https://pkgload.r-lib.org/reference/load_all.html) +
render the `\value`, not full `R CMD check`; `devtools` is NOT in the
renv lib, `roxygen2`/`pkgload`/`pkgbuild` ARE\]. **Apply:** any task
editing roxygen/generated docs ‚Äî check the regen blast radius, keep
tooling migrations out of content fixes, and re-verify every inherited
claim firsthand before acting on it.

#### Learning 107 ‚Äî A generated-tooling version migration, taken as its OWN deliverable, is the clean inverse of the blast-radius trap (Learning 106): the diff is small and reviewable, and regenerating with the newer tool also REPAIRS content that had silently drifted between the source and the committed artifact. Adopt it safely by reading the COMPLETE regen diff (every change must be an intended kind) and confirming the new tool introduces ZERO new linter findings ‚Äî not by spot-checking. (S113, owner pick A ‚Äî adopted roxygen2 8.0.0, regenerated `man/`)

**What happened.** S112 hit roxygen2 8.0.0 reformatting `man/` mid-way
through a content fix and correctly isolated the version migration as a
separate, deliberate task. S113 executed that task as its whole
deliverable. Counter to the ‚Äúblast radius‚Äù framing, the isolated
migration was *small*: **26 of 190 `.Rd` files + `DESCRIPTION`**, 31
insertions / 29 deletions, `NAMESPACE` untouched. The changes were
exactly three kinds ‚Äî the `Config/roxygen2/version` field rename
(`RoxygenNote: 7.3.2` retired), 24 dataset docs adopting 8.0.0‚Äôs
canonical `\usage{ data(name) }` form, and a cosmetic `\value` re-wrap ‚Äî
plus a fourth, unexpected and *beneficial*: regenerating
`man/nprcgenekeepr-package.Rd` repaired a shipped typo (`'mulatto'` ‚Üí
`'mulatta'`, already correct in `DESCRIPTION`) and added the maintainer
to the Authors list. The committed 7.3.2 `.Rd` had drifted from its
source; the regen reconciled it.

**The migration IS the point, so verification is ‚Äúis the WHOLE diff
intended?‚Äù, not ‚Äúdid I break anything?‚Äù** The adversarial check that
mattered was reading the complete diff (all 27 files) and tallying every
added line ‚Äî confirming there were no surprise edits hiding among the
mechanical ones ‚Äî and proving the new tool added ZERO new `checkRd`
problems by checking all 26 pages and confirming the 11 flagged
`\title`-period NOTEs were already present on the committed HEAD (62 of
190 package-wide). A spot-check of a few files would have missed either
a stray change or mistaken a pre-existing NOTE for a regression.

**Right-sizing: SOLO, but with a full-diff audit, not a fan-out.** A
deterministic regen whose diff is fully inspectable has no competing
claims to adjudicate, so a multi-agent workflow would be theater (the
S111/S106 call). The rigor lived in reading 100% of the diff and the
HEAD-vs-working `checkRd` comparison, not in parallelism. Pinning the
old tool was the rejected alternative: roxygen2 isn‚Äôt in `renv.lock`
(dev-only dep), so a pin needs a manual downgrade and doesn‚Äôt survive
[`renv::restore`](https://rstudio.github.io/renv/reference/restore.html)
‚Äî adopting was both less work and durable.

**Reflexes:** \[a tooling version migration is a legitimate standalone
deliverable ‚Äî isolate it from content fixes (Learning 106), then DO it
deliberately when it‚Äôs the chosen task\]\[verify a regen by reading the
COMPLETE diff and classifying every change as an intended kind ‚Äî a
regen‚Äôs correctness is ‚Äúis the whole diff intended?‚Äù, not ‚Äúspot-check
looks fine‚Äù\]\[prove a new codegen tool adds no regressions by running
the linter (`checkRd`) on every changed artifact AND confirming any
findings pre-exist on HEAD ‚Äî don‚Äôt assume a NOTE on a changed file is
new\]\[a newer codegen tool can repair drift between source and a stale
committed artifact ‚Äî treat it as a benefit, but disclose it (it
ships)\]\[prefer adopting over pinning when the tool isn‚Äôt snapshotted
in the lock ‚Äî a pin isn‚Äôt durable across
[`renv::restore`](https://rstudio.github.io/renv/reference/restore.html)\].
**Apply:** any deliberate regeneration-tool or formatter version bump
(roxygen2, styler, a codegen) ‚Äî take it as its own commit, audit the
entire diff, and confirm zero new linter findings against HEAD.

#### Learning 108 ‚Äî A large-but-uniform mechanical doc sweep (here 62 `\title`-ends-in-period `checkRd` NOTEs) is a legitimate SINGLE deliverable when the edit is ONE kind and the verification is an ORACLE: dry-run a deterministic script and audit every before/after BEFORE writing, then prove via a per-page HEAD-vs-working `checkRd` diff that ONLY the target NOTE disappeared and NOTHING was added. (S114, owner pick A2 ‚Äî title-period mop-up across 52 title files + 3 GV `@return` files = 55 `R/` total)

**What happened.** The flagged work ‚Äî 62 of 190 `.Rd` carrying
`\title should not end in a period`, plus `calcFE`‚Äôs spurious `r` clause
and the unbalanced-paren `fg` formula in `calcFEFG`/`calcFG` ‚Äî all
re-verified true against the current tree (a flag is a claim, not a
fact; Learning 105/106). The blast radius (~55 source files) *looks*
like it violates the SAFEGUARDS ‚Äú5 files‚Äù rule, but the rule targets
unrelated multi-file scope creep; a single uniform mechanical change
(remove one trailing period per title) verified by an oracle is a
different shape. Right-sized as ONE deliverable after the owner blessed
the scope via a pre-RED `AskUserQuestion` (full sweep vs.¬Ýnarrower;
concrete previews; ASCII-only labels per
\[\[ascii-only-in-question-options\]\]).

**Why SOLO + script + oracle, not a fan-out.**
[`tools::checkRd()`](https://rdrr.io/r/tools/checkRd.html) is a
ground-truth oracle: if 0 title-period NOTEs remain and no changed page
gained a new note, the titles are correct. When the check is an oracle,
parallel agents add variance, not safety, for ‚Äúremove one character‚Äù ‚Äî a
deterministic script is *more* reliable than 51 hand-edits or 51
sub-agents. Edits were made at the roxygen SOURCE and `man/` regenerated
(never hand-edit `.Rd`). The rigor lived in two places: (1) a
**dry-run** of the title script that printed every title paragraph + its
before/after so all 51 were audited *before* any write (each correctly
identified the title = first roxygen paragraph, including multi-line
titles); (2) a **per-page HEAD-vs-working `checkRd` comparison**
(normalize away line numbers, set-diff the messages) proving 62 pages
had ONLY the period NOTE removed and 0 pages gained anything ‚Äî the
precise ‚Äúno new problems‚Äù proof, scoped to the 65 changed pages (the
other 125 are byte-identical to HEAD). Confirmed `load_all` (162
exports) + `NAMESPACE`/`DESCRIPTION` unchanged. This extends Learning
107 (deterministic regen ‚Üí SOLO full-diff audit) to a deterministic
*source sweep*.

**The shell backslash trap (recurring ‚Äî bit S112/S113 too).**
`Rscript -e '... pattern="\\.Rd$" ...'` and `grep("^\\\\title", ...)`
get mangled through the Bash tool layer ‚Äî a backslash level is stripped,
so R sees an invalid escape (`'\.' is an unrecognized escape`) or a `\t`
tab that matches nothing. Fix: WRITE the R probe to a file (Write tool)
and run `Rscript file.R` ‚Äî file contents are passed verbatim, no shell
re-escaping ‚Äî and/or use backslash-free constructs: `endsWith(f, ".Rd")`
not a regex, `[.]` not `\\.`, `fixed = TRUE`, POSIX `[[:space:]]`. Reach
for the file-based probe FIRST given the documented history.

**Scope discipline.** Period-removal is mechanical, so it left adjacent
CONTENT bugs intact, which were FLAGGED not fixed (FM \#8):
`findPedigreeNumber`‚Äôs title is a copy-paste of `findGeneration`‚Äôs;
`pedMissingBirth`/`pedSameMaleIsSireAndDam` data docs claim ‚Äúno errors‚Äù
though they are error-demo sets; `focalAnimals`/`convertSexCodes`
grammar. The minimal-vs-proper fork for data-doc titles (remove period
vs.¬Ýrewrite the run-on into a short title + `@description`) was surfaced
to the owner as part of the scope question; the owner chose minimal, so
the long titles are now checkRd-clean but a proper rewrite is deferred.

**Reflexes:** \[a large-but-UNIFORM mechanical doc change is one
deliverable when the edit is a single kind AND a linter/oracle can
confirm it ‚Äî the SAFEGUARDS ‚Äú5 files‚Äù rule targets unrelated scope
creep, not one verified uniform sweep\]\[prefer a deterministic script
over hand-edits or sub-agents when the change is ‚Äúremove/replace one
token‚Äù ‚Äî but DRY-RUN it and audit every before/after before
writing\]\[prove ‚Äúno regressions‚Äù by a per-page HEAD-vs-working linter
diff (normalize line numbers, set-diff messages), scoped to changed
files ‚Äî don‚Äôt eyeball\]\[when a linter is a ground-truth oracle, SOLO +
script + oracle beats a fan-out; parallel agents add variance not safety
for trivial mechanical edits\]\[write R probes to a FILE to dodge the
recurring shell backslash-mangling trap, or use
`endsWith`/`[.]`/`fixed=TRUE`/POSIX classes\]\[edit roxygen SOURCE
titles + regenerate; never hand-edit `.Rd`\]\[period-removal leaves
CONTENT bugs intact ‚Äî flag the copy-paste/garbled titles, don‚Äôt fix them
in a mechanical pass\]. **Apply:** any mechanical lint-cleanup sweep
(trailing periods, deprecated tags, formatting) across many files ‚Äî pose
the scope to the owner, dry-run + audit a deterministic fixer,
regenerate, and prove via a per-file before/after linter diff that only
the target finding cleared and nothing new appeared.

#### Learning 109 ‚Äî A flagged doc ‚Äúbug‚Äù is often one token inside a larger wrong claim; the correct fix verifies and repairs the WHOLE sentence against ground-truth data, and a regenerated data-doc‚Äôs auto-computed `\format` block is a free oracle for shape claims. (S115, owner pick A ‚Äî repaired the 4 content bugs S114 flagged)

**What happened.** S114 flagged `pedMissingBirth`‚Äôs data doc for saying
‚Äúrepresenting a full pedigree with no errors‚Äù (it is an error-demo set).
Loading the data to verify (Learning 105/106) showed the demonstrated
error is that the `birth_date` column is ABSENT entirely ‚Äî and that the
same sentence‚Äôs ‚Äú5 columns (ego_id, sire, dam_id, sex, birth_date)‚Äù was
therefore ALSO wrong (the object has 4 columns). The flagged token (‚Äúno
errors‚Äù) sat inside a larger inaccurate claim; fixing only the token
would have left ‚Äúmissing the birth_date column‚Äù contradicting ‚Äú5 columns
including birth_date‚Äù in the same sentence. Surfaced the fork (minimal
touch vs.¬Ýfull accuracy) to the owner via `AskUserQuestion` grounded in
the probe output (concrete before/after previews, ASCII-only labels per
\[\[ascii-only-in-question-options\]\]); owner chose full accuracy. The
regenerated `.Rd`‚Äôs auto-generated `\format` line already read ‚Äú8 rows
and 4 columns‚Äù ‚Äî independent corroboration that the hand-written ‚Äú5
columns‚Äù prose had drifted from the data object.

**Verify the claim, not the flag.** All four flags re-checked against
ground truth before editing: `pedSameMaleIsSireAndDam` row `o3` has
`dam_id = s1` (a male) and `s1` sires `o1`/`o2` (the same male is both
sire and dam; 5 columns correct, so only the error clause changed);
`focalAnimals` is 1 column (`id`) / 327 rows; `findPedigreeNumber`
assigns a connected-component number (the `pedNum` vector), not a
generation (its title was a verbatim copy of `findGeneration`‚Äôs);
`convertSexCodes`‚Äôs ‚Äúto a standardized codes‚Äù is a number-agreement
error. Each fix states what the data/function actually is.

**A mid-session owner constraint can be already-satisfied ‚Äî confirm,
don‚Äôt re-do.** The owner interjected that `pedMissingBirth` must ‚Äúretain
the characteristic of not having a Birth column.‚Äù Because the
deliverable touched only documentation (never `data/`), the constraint
was already met; the right response was to verify the data object
post-edit (4 columns, `birth_date` absent) and confirm, not to change
anything (FM \#23 inverse ‚Äî don‚Äôt manufacture work from a constraint
that‚Äôs already honored).

**Right-sizing: SOLO with firsthand data probes + a `checkRd` oracle.**
Five deterministic prose fixes whose correctness is established by
printing the actual data objects ‚Äî a fan-out of agents to re-print data
I already printed would be theater (the S111/S113/S114 call).
Verification = per-page `checkRd` HEAD-vs-working diff (0 problems, 0
new) + `load_all` (162 exports) + a `data/`-unchanged check. Edits at
the roxygen SOURCE, `man/` regenerated; never hand-edit `.Rd`. Used
file-based R probes from the first command, dodging the recurring
shell-backslash trap (Learning 108) that bit S112‚ÄìS114.

**Scope discipline.** Fixed exactly the four flagged items; FLAGGED but
did not fix newly-discovered adjacent bugs (FM \#8): the
`\code{pedgood}` wrong-case cross-reference in all six error-set docs
(dataset is `pedGood`), the `si.re`-vs-`sire` data/doc column-name
mismatch across the qc data family, and roxygen 8.0.0‚Äôs
`@importFrom must be only 1 line long` errors in `mod*.R`.

**Reflexes:** \[a flagged doc bug is a claim about one token ‚Äî verify
the WHOLE surrounding sentence against ground-truth data, because the
correct fix often must repair a second, unflagged inaccuracy for
coherence\]\[load the actual data object and read its columns/rows
before describing a dataset ‚Äî names lie, `\format` is computed\]\[a
regenerated data-doc‚Äôs auto-generated `\format` block is a free oracle
for shape claims (row/column counts) ‚Äî cross-check hand-written prose
against it\]\[a mid-session owner constraint may already be satisfied by
the deliverable‚Äôs scope ‚Äî verify and confirm, don‚Äôt re-open
work\]\[SOLO + firsthand probes + `checkRd` oracle for deterministic
prose fixes; flag (don‚Äôt fix) adjacent bugs found along the way\].
**Apply:** any task fixing a flagged documentation error ‚Äî re-verify the
flag against data, repair the entire inaccurate claim (not just the
flagged word), cross-check counts against the generated `\format`, and
flag adjacent bugs for a separate pass.

#### Learning 110 ‚Äî A flag can be wrong about a bug‚Äôs NATURE and SCOPE, not just its existence; before fixing, disprove your own hypothesis with the oracle, scan the whole codebase for the pattern, and trace ‚Äúmessy‚Äù data through its consumer ‚Äî the principled fix sometimes INVERTS the obvious one. (S116, owner pick A ‚Äî fixed the 3 adjacent doc/data-doc bugs S115 flagged)

**What happened.** S116 inherited three flags from S115 and each needed
re-examination beyond ‚Äúis it real?‚Äù: (1) **Nature was wrong.** S115‚Äôs
flag ‚Äî and my own restatement ‚Äî called the wrapped `@importFrom` tags a
‚Äúlatent NAMESPACE-drop hazard.‚Äù A fully-reverted regen probe
(`roxygenise()` on the current source, then `git diff NAMESPACE` +
count, then `git checkout`) disproved it: roxygen 8.0.0 prints
`‚úñ @importFrom must be only 1 line long` for all of them but still
parses every continuation line ‚Äî `NAMESPACE` came back byte-identical
(140 `importFrom`, 0 removed). The bug was cosmetic lint, not a
regression. (2) **Scope was undercounted 3√ó.** The flag named 3 `mod*.R`
files; a file-based detector scanning every `@importFrom` in `R/`
(checking whether the next roxygen line is a non-tag continuation) found
**20 wrapped tags across 10 files**, including non-`mod` files
(`appServer.R`, `appUI.R`). (3) **The obvious fix was inverted.**
`si.re` (and `pedOne`‚Äôs `si re`) looked like a corrupted `sire` to
rename in the data; tracing it through its consumer showed
`fixColumnNames` strips spaces then periods (`si re` ‚Üí `si.re` ‚Üí `sire`)
and `qcStudbook(pedGood)` returns canonical `sire` ‚Äî so `si.re` is an
*intentional raw QC-demo fixture* (`make.names("si re")` at `.rda`
creation), and renaming it would gut the fixture it exists to exercise.
The correct fix was the docs, not the data.

**Disprove your own hypothesis with the oracle, cheaply and
reversibly.** The single most valuable step was a throwaway regen whose
entire output I reverted (`git checkout -- man/ NAMESPACE DESCRIPTION`).
It converted a plausible-sounding inherited claim (‚Äúhazard‚Äù) into a
measured fact (‚Äúcosmetic; NAMESPACE byte-identical‚Äù) before I touched
anything ‚Äî and I reported the correction to the owner rather than
quietly proceeding. Verify-the-claim-not-the-flag (Learning 105/106/109)
applies to *your own* characterization too, not just to a predecessor‚Äôs.

**Trace ‚Äúmessy‚Äù data through its consumer before cleaning it.** A value
that looks like corruption (`si.re`) can be a deliberate test input.
Read the normalization/validation function (`fixColumnNames`) and run
the end-to-end consumer (`qcStudbook`) to see what the messy form is
*for*. If it round-trips to the canonical value, it‚Äôs load-bearing ‚Äî fix
the description, not the datum.

**Two owner-decisions ‚Üí two pre-RED gates, each grounded in evidence.**
The data-vs-doc fork (item 2) and the 3-vs-10-file boundary (item 3,
surfaced *after* the scope/nature discovery) were each posed as a
separate `AskUserQuestion` with a recommendation and concrete tradeoffs
(ASCII-only labels per \[\[ascii-only-in-question-options\]\]); owner
chose full-accuracy docs and all-10-files. A discovery that changes what
a chosen option *means* (scope tripled, hazard‚Üícosmetic) warrants
re-surfacing, not silent expansion (FM \#8) or a silent half-fix.

**Prove the neutral claim with the oracle, and match surrounding
style.** Item 3‚Äôs safety rested on `NAMESPACE` being byte-identical
after the real regen (`git diff --quiet -- NAMESPACE`), not on
reasoning. And the reformat split each over-long list into *multiple*
single-line `@importFrom pkg ...` tags wrapped at ‚â§80 chars (a
deterministic dry-run-first script, audited before writing, per Learning
108) ‚Äî matching the authors‚Äô existing wrapping rather than collapsing to
one 180-char line. Regression read confirmed 0 new failures (no
executable line changed ‚Äî only `#'` comments).

**Reflexes:** \[run a reverted regen/build probe to measure a codegen
tool‚Äôs ACTUAL behavior before believing a flag about its effect ‚Äî ‚Äú‚úñ‚Äù
lint is not the same as a functional change\]\[scan the whole codebase
for the flagged pattern with a deterministic detector ‚Äî a flag‚Äôs file
list is a sample, not an inventory (SESSION_RUNNER
evidence-based-inventory applies to bug-fix scope too)\]\[before
‚Äúcleaning‚Äù a value that looks corrupt, read its consumer/normalizer and
run it end-to-end ‚Äî an ugly raw value may be an intentional fixture that
round-trips to canonical\]\[correct your OWN mis-characterization to the
owner and re-pose scope when a discovery changes what the chosen option
means\]\[prove a ‚Äúwon‚Äôt change X‚Äù claim with the byte-level oracle
(`git diff --quiet`), not reasoning\]\[reformat to match surrounding
style ‚Äî multiple ‚â§80-char single-line tags, not one mega-line\].
**Apply:** any inherited-flag fix touching generated artifacts
(roxygen/NAMESPACE/codegen) or example/test data ‚Äî probe the tool‚Äôs real
behavior, inventory the pattern across the repo, trace messy data
through its consumer, gate genuine owner-forks, and verify neutrality
with the artifact oracle.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

#### Learning 111 ‚Äî For a doc-rewrite that is structurally oracle-checkable but whose QUALITY is not, split the verification: do the edits SOLO with firsthand probes + oracle (checkRd/load_all/tests), then fan out an adversarial critic panel for the subjective dimension (title aptness, completeness, cross-doc consistency) that no oracle can judge. (S117, owner pick A2 ‚Äî short-`@title` rewrite of all 24 data docs)

**What happened.** The deliverable was rewriting all 24 `R/data.R`
data-doc titles from ‚ÄúX is a ‚Ä¶‚Äù run-ons into short noun-phrase titles,
with the detail moved to `@description`. Structural correctness ‚Äî does
it render, is it `checkRd`-clean, are `NAMESPACE`/exports/tests
unchanged? ‚Äî is fully oracle-decidable, so fanning *that* out would be
theater (Learnings 108‚Äì110). But title *quality* ‚Äî is ‚ÄúExample studbook
with sex-mismatched parents‚Äù apt, accurate, non-duplicative? ‚Äî is
**not** oracle-checkable. So I made the 24 edits solo (serial edits to
one file) and verified structure with the oracle, then ran a 3-lens
critic workflow (accuracy / completeness / style+consistency) over the
24 before/after pairs, each lens grounding itself in
`git show HEAD:R/data.R` plus the loaded objects. It returned **0 block,
0 should-fix, 3 nits**: a pre-existing `recordStats`‚Üí`recordStatus`
`\describe` drift (flagged, out of scope, FM \#8), a confusable
near-duplicate title prefix (`smallPed` vs `lacy1989Ped` ‚Äî fix applied),
and a studbook-vs-pedigree term split (kept ‚Äî owner-approved in the
preview).

**Verify every count-bearing claim before authoring it; a
self-contradicting auto-`\format` is a free oracle.** Before moving any
‚ÄúN rows and M columns‚Äù sentence into a description I checked each
against the live object. Exactly one was wrong: `qcPed` said ‚Äú277 rows
and 6 columns‚Äù but is 280√ó8 ‚Äî and roxygen‚Äôs auto-generated `\format`
already read ‚Äú280 rows and 8 columns‚Äù, so the page literally
contradicted itself. Authoring the moved sentence verbatim would have
perpetuated a falsehood; I wrote the accurate 280√ó8 and flagged the
correction (the S115/S116 verify-and-correct pattern, now extended to
title-rewrites).

**A critic suggestion that reverses an owner-approved previewed choice
is a note, not a mandate; improving your own un-previewed draft is
within latitude.** The style lens flagged ‚Äústudbook‚Äù (the 6 QC fixtures)
as inconsistent with the file‚Äôs dominant ‚Äúpedigree‚Äù ‚Äî but the owner had
explicitly selected the ‚Äústudbook‚Äù preview when choosing the style, so I
kept it (defensible: studbook-style input fixtures that *represent*
pedigrees and feed `qcStudbook`) and recorded the observation for the
future rather than silently flipping approved text. The `smallPed`
near-duplicate, by contrast, was my own un-previewed draft ‚Äî I applied
the disambiguation directly.

**Reflexes:** \[for a rewrite whose structure is oracle-checkable but
whose quality is not, do edits solo+oracle and fan out ONLY the
subjective dimension ‚Äî that‚Äôs the non-theater use of a critic
panel\]\[give each critic lens ground truth (HEAD diff + loaded
objects), not your summary, so it verifies independently\]\[verify every
count/column claim against the live object before moving it into prose;
a self-contradicting auto-`\format` is a free oracle\]\[keep an
owner-approved previewed choice when a critic merely *prefers* an
alternative ‚Äî note it, don‚Äôt flip it; freely improve your own
un-previewed drafts\]\[flag pre-existing adjacent content bugs the panel
surfaces (FM \#8), don‚Äôt fix them mid-task\]. **Apply:** any bulk
documentation/content rewrite where rendering + lint prove structure but
not aptness ‚Äî pair solo oracle verification with an adversarial quality
panel grounded in source-of-truth, verify factual claims firsthand, and
respect the owner-decision boundary.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

#### Learning 112 ‚Äî ‚ÄúMerge X to master‚Äù is about `origin/master`, not the local `master` branch ‚Äî `git fetch` first and reason about the REMOTE topology; a local mainline branch can be far behind, and the repo‚Äôs real merge mechanism (PR vs direct push) is encoded in its history. (S118, owner pick C ‚Äî merge `add-methodology` ‚Üí `master`)

**What happened.** Asked to ‚Äúmerge add-methodology to master‚Äù, the naive
read was a local fast-forward: local `master` (4790b64f) was a clean
ancestor of `add-methodology`, 183 behind. But `git branch -vv` showed
local `master` as `[origin/master: behind 168]` ‚Äî the LOCAL master was a
stale red herring; the real target is `origin/master` (7a8433b3 = ‚ÄúMerge
pull request \#51 from rmsharp/add-methodology‚Äù). After `git fetch`,
`add-methodology` and `origin/master` were DIVERGED: 18 ahead / 3 behind
‚Äî and the 3 ‚Äúbehind‚Äù commits are merely the PR \#41/#43/#51 merge
bubbles (master accumulates merge commits; add-methodology stays linear
and never pulls them back). Fork point `14032640` (S100); the unmerged
work was S101‚ÄìS117 (18 commits). The repo‚Äôs established mechanism is
GitHub PRs (a commit on add-methodology, 6175fe66, even records ‚ÄúPR \#51
merged to master‚Äù). So the correct action was a fresh PR \#52, not a
local merge.

**Fetch before reasoning about remote branches; trust `origin/<branch>`,
not local.** Remote-tracking refs are only as fresh as the last fetch,
and a local mainline branch can sit hundreds of commits behind its
remote (here 168). The first move for any ‚Äúmerge/compare/rebase against
master‚Äù task is `git fetch`, then reason about `origin/master`. The
`[behind N]` annotation in `git branch -vv` is the tell that a local
branch is stale.

**Read the merge mechanism out of the history before choosing one.** A
run of ‚ÄúMerge pull request \#N from ‚Äù commits on the target ‚Äî plus a
feature branch that stays linear and never contains those merge nodes ‚Äî
means the project merges via PRs and never reconciles the feature branch
backward. Matching that (open PR \#52) keeps history consistent; a local
`--no-ff` merge + direct push would deviate and may hit branch
protection.

**Confirm the mechanism on an outward main-branch action even under an
explicit instruction.** ‚ÄúMerge to master‚Äù authorizes the goal, but
pushing/merging the main branch is hard to reverse and the *who-merges*
split is a real choice ‚Äî a one-question gated `AskUserQuestion` (I-merge
vs you-merge vs local) resolved it (owner: push + open PR, owner
merges). \[\[ascii-only-in-question-options\]\]

**Reflexes:** \[`git fetch` before comparing against any remote branch;
reason about `origin/<branch>`, never a possibly-stale local
mainline\]\[a `[behind N]` in `git branch -vv` means the local branch is
a red herring ‚Äî use the remote\]\[detect PR-based vs direct-push
workflow from the merge-commit pattern in the target‚Äôs history and match
it\]\[on an outward main-branch action, confirm the mechanism and
who-merges with one gated question even when the goal is explicitly
authorized\]. **Apply:** any ‚Äúmerge/sync/compare branch X into mainline‚Äù
task ‚Äî fetch first, target `origin/<mainline>`, identify the established
merge mechanism from history, and gate the outward step.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

#### Learning 113 ‚Äî When the task is to fix an AUTHORIZED SET of flagged bugs, treat that list itself as a sample, not an inventory: after fixing exactly the authorized items, run a completeness scan of the WHOLE artifact for the same bug CLASS and FLAG (don‚Äôt fix) the siblings. (S120, owner pick A ‚Äî fixed the 3 adjacent data-doc content bugs flagged in S117)

**What happened.** The owner authorized fixing exactly three
S117-flagged data-doc content bugs in `R/data.R`: `examplePedigree`‚Äôs
`\item{recordStats}` ‚Üí `\item{recordStatus}` (real 12th column is
`recordStatus`, `recordStats` absent); `rhesusGenotypes`‚Äôs garbled
‚ÄúThere are object.‚Äù ‚Üí ‚ÄúThere are 31 rows and 3 columns.‚Äù (`dim` = 31√ó3,
31 unique ids, consistent with the neighboring ‚ÄúRepresents 31 animals‚Äù
and the auto-`\format`); and `exampleNprcgenekeeprConfig`‚Äôs ‚Äú‚Ä¶file
created the SNPRC.‚Äù ‚Üí ‚Äú‚Ä¶created at the SNPRC.‚Äù (missing locative
preposition). Each was verified against the live object before authoring
(Learning 109/111), edited at the roxygen source, regenerated (3 `man/`
pages, `NAMESPACE` byte-identical), and adversarially confirmed by three
independent refutation agents. But the highest-value step was a
**completeness critic** that scanned all 24 `\docType{data}` blocks
against their loaded objects for the same *class* of bug ‚Äî and found
**two more not in the authorized list**: `ped1Alleles`‚Äôs V2/V3/V4
`\item`s all say ‚Äúiteration 1‚Äù though V1‚âÝV2‚âÝV3‚âÝV4 and the block itself
says ‚Äú4 iterations‚Äù (R/data.R 134‚Äì142), and a missing-space
‚Äúcolumn.Unknown dams‚Äù in the `dam` item of
`examplePedigree`/`lacy1989Ped`/`rhesusPedigree` whose parallel `sire`
items are correctly spaced (R/data.R 24/97/365). Both confirmed
firsthand, then **flagged for a future session, not fixed** (FM \#8, ‚Äú1
and done‚Äù).

**An authorized list of flags is a sample of a bug class.** Learning 110
established that a single flag‚Äôs *file list* is a sample of one
pattern‚Äôs scope. This extends it one level up: a *set* of authorized
flags is a sample of a bug *class*. Fixing only the named items leaves
same-kind siblings in the artifact. A cheap whole-artifact scan for the
class ‚Äî grounded in the live data (garbled/templated fragments,
`\item{}` names that aren‚Äôt real columns, grammar gaps, prose counts vs
[`dim()`](https://rdrr.io/r/base/dim.html)) ‚Äî turns ‚Äúfixed what I was
told‚Äù into ‚Äúfound everything of this kind.‚Äù Scope discipline means you
FLAG the extras, you do not fix them.

**Right-sizing under ultracode (where the fan-out is non-theater vs
theater).** Three one-line fixes whose structural correctness is
oracle-decidable (`checkRd`/`load_all`/regression/`NAMESPACE` byte-diff)
were edited SOLO+oracle. The workflow‚Äôs agents added information the
oracle cannot: independent re-derivation of each fix‚Äôs ground truth
(adversarial refutation) and the class-completeness scan. Honestly,
three identical verifiers re-deriving dims I already derived sit near
the theater line (Learnings 108‚Äì110); the **completeness scan is the
part that earned the fan-out** ‚Äî it found two real bugs the flag-list
missed.

**Gate only the genuine fork.** Two of the three fixes were fully
determined by ground truth (a real column name; a missing preposition
S117 had already diagnosed) and needed no question. Only the garbled
`rhesusGenotypes` fragment had \>1 reasonable repair (fill in the real
count vs.¬Ýdelete the redundant sentence), so that single editorial
choice was the one gated `AskUserQuestion` (owner: fill in). Determined
fixes do not need a gate; over-gating is its own failure.

**Reflexes:** \[treat an authorized SET of flagged bugs as a sample of a
bug CLASS ‚Äî after fixing the named items, scan the whole artifact for
the same class against live data and FLAG the siblings (extends Learning
110‚Äôs ‚Äúa flag‚Äôs file list is a sample‚Äù)\]\[the completeness critic
grounded in live data is the non-theater half of a verify workflow for a
fix task ‚Äî adversarial re-derivation confirms YOUR fixes, the scan finds
what the flag-list missed\]\[gate ONLY the genuine editorial fork ‚Äî a
fix determined by ground truth (a real column, a real dim, a missing
word) needs no question; a garbled fragment with \>1 reasonable repair
does\]\[verify every flagged item firsthand before putting it in a
handoff ‚Äî a flag must be confirmed, not relayed\]. **Apply:** any task
that fixes a list of flagged content/doc bugs ‚Äî verify each against
ground truth, gate only genuine forks, fix exactly the authorized set,
then run a class-completeness scan of the whole artifact and flag (don‚Äôt
fix) the adjacents.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

#### Learning 114 ‚Äî Verify a completeness-scanner‚Äôs flag firsthand against ALL parallel siblings ‚Äî a per-group scanner‚Äôs finding is itself a sample and can miss a same-text sibling in another scan group. And when one bug CLASS recurs across many sessions, propose a single batch sweep as the better deliverable. (S121, owner pick A4 ‚Äî fixed the 2 same-class data-doc bugs S120 flagged)

**What happened.** (A4) fixed exactly the two bugs S120‚Äôs completeness
scan had flagged: `ped1Alleles` `\item{V2}/{V3}/{V4}` ‚Äúiteration 1‚Äù ‚Üí
2/3/4 (object 554√ó6; V1‚ÄìV4 distinct ‚Äî all 6 pairwise
[`identical()`](https://rdrr.io/r/base/identical.html)=FALSE, 290/554
rows differ; `\item{parent}` says ‚Äú4 iterations‚Äù; V1 correctly stays
‚Äúiteration 1‚Äù), and the missing-space ‚Äúcolumn.Unknown dams‚Äù in three
`dam` items. Both were ground-truth-determined ‚Üí no gate (Learning 113),
edited SOLO+oracle (checkRd 0√ó4, NAMESPACE byte-identical, load_all 162,
regression 0/0, rendered-`.Rd` diff matches intent), and adversarially
CONFIRMED (2 refuters, refuted 0/2 each). The fresh completeness scan (4
agents over the 24 data docs) flagged a third same-class bug ‚Äî
`examplePedigree`‚Äôs `\item{sex}` claims levels ‚ÄúM,F,U‚Äù but the factor is
`F,M,H,U`.

**A completeness-scanner‚Äôs flag is a sample too ‚Äî verify it firsthand
against every parallel sibling.** Before writing the sex-levels flag
into the handoff I probed every data object‚Äôs `sex` column and grepped
every doc claiming ‚Äúfactor with levels‚Äù. The scanner (which only saw its
own 6-doc group) flagged `examplePedigree` but MISSED that
`rhesusPedigree` carries the identical doc text and is ALSO wrong ‚Äî in
the opposite direction (its factor is just `F,M`; the doc over-claims a
`U` level that doesn‚Äôt exist). Firsthand verification turned a one-doc
flag into the correct two-doc scope. This is Learning 113‚Äôs ‚Äúa flag is a
sample‚Äù applied to the verification agent‚Äôs OWN output: a fan-out
partitioned by group is structurally blind to a cross-group sibling, so
confirm-firsthand must check the whole sibling set (every doc sharing
the claim), not just the flagged instance.

**When a bug CLASS keeps recurring, the one-bug-at-a-time cadence is the
wrong shape ‚Äî propose a batch sweep.** Data-doc factual-accuracy bugs
have now been found and fixed across S114/S115/S116/S117/S120/S121
(counts, column names, item labels, prose typos, and now factor levels).
Each session fixes the named instance and the completeness scan finds
the next. That pattern says the higher-ROI deliverable is a single
owner-gated sweep that audits EVERY factual claim in all 24 data docs
against the live objects (dims, column names/order, factor levels,
counts, prose) and fixes them as one batch ‚Äî rather than discovering one
more each session. Flagged as a suggested next deliverable (not started
‚Äî ‚Äú1 and done‚Äù).

**Reflexes:** \[a completeness-scanner‚Äôs finding is a sample ‚Äî before
relaying it, verify firsthand against ALL parallel siblings (other docs
sharing the same claim text), because a group-partitioned fan-out is
blind to cross-group siblings\]\[when the same bug CLASS surfaces across
many sessions, stop fixing one-at-a-time and propose a single batch
audit-and-fix sweep as the deliverable\]\[both fixes determined by
ground truth ‚Üí no gate (Learning 113); adversarial refuters confirm YOUR
fixes, the scan finds the next sibling\]. **Apply:** any session fixing
flagged content/doc bugs whose verification fans out by group ‚Äî confirm
each flag against the whole sibling set firsthand, and if the bug class
is recurring, recommend a batch sweep instead of serial one-offs.

#### Learning 115 ‚Äî The batch sweep that Learning 114 proposed paid off: one oracle-grounded audit of all 24 data docs found 10 discrepancies where the serial cadence found ~1-2/session. An accuracy audit also surfaces a 2nd-order class ‚Äî ‚Äúdata-artifact‚Äù bugs (the data itself looks degraded), which is the OWNER‚Äôs call, not the auditor‚Äôs ‚Äî and the adversarial pass must BOUND scope (reject non-claims), not just confirm. (S122, owner pick A5 promoted to a full 24-doc audit + fix)

**What happened.** S121‚Äôs Learning 114 recommended replacing the
one-bug-at-a-time data-doc cadence (S114‚ÄìS121) with a single owner-gated
sweep. Owner picked it. I computed authoritative ground truth for all 24
objects once
([`pkgload::load_all`](https://pkgload.r-lib.org/reference/load_all.html) +
direct inspection), then ran a workflow: 5 independent lens scanners
(dims/counts, column-names, types/levels, prose/crossref, free-roam)
over all 24 docs for completeness, then an adversarial refuter per
unique discrepancy. Result: **12 candidates ‚Üí 10 confirmed, 2
rejected**, fixed in one pass (9 doc edits across 4 objects; checkRd
0√ó4, NAMESPACE byte-identical, load_all 162, regression 0/0,
rendered-`.Rd` diffs match intent). The serial sessions had been
clearing ~1‚Äì2 of these per session; the batch found the whole remaining
set at once. **The recurring-class signal was right ‚Äî when a bug class
keeps reappearing, the fix is a class-complete sweep, not another single
fix.**

**An accuracy audit surfaces a 2nd-order bug class the auditor must NOT
silently resolve ‚Äî gate it.** Seven findings were plain ‚Äúdoc-bugs‚Äù (the
object is legitimate; the doc is wrong ‚Äî e.g.¬Ý`sex` factor levels
`"M","F","U"` vs the live `F,M,H,U`; `ancestry` documented as free-form
character but actually a closed factor; `id`/`qcBreeders` mistyped). But
two were different in KIND: `rhesusPedigree`‚Äôs `birth` ships as a
factor-of-strings (not Date) and `exit` as all-NA logical (no dates at
all) ‚Äî the *data itself* looks degraded by obfuscation, so ‚Äúmake the doc
match the object‚Äù would enshrine what may be a data defect. That is the
owner‚Äôs decision (document the degraded reality now vs.¬Ýleave the
aspirational doc and queue a data re-export), not a default the auditor
should pick. I split the deliverable: fixed the 7 unambiguous doc-bugs,
and posed ONE focused gate for the 2 data-artifacts (owner chose
‚Äúdocument actual types now‚Äù). **Naming the doc-bug vs data-artifact
distinction is what turns an over-reaching ‚ÄúI rewrote everything to
match‚Äù into a faithful audit.**

**The adversarial pass must BOUND scope, not just confirm.** The
refuters didn‚Äôt only re-derive and confirm my candidates ‚Äî they REJECTED
two (`rhesusPedigree` `sire`/`dam`), correctly ruling that those items
make no type claim at all (‚Äúthe male/female parent ‚Ä¶ NA for unknown‚Äù),
so ‚Äúcorrecting‚Äù them to say ‚Äúfactor‚Äù would invent a fix for a non-bug.
In a fix-audit the skeptic‚Äôs job is symmetric: confirm real mismatches
AND veto claimed mismatches that aren‚Äôt, so the sweep doesn‚Äôt
manufacture churn on accurate-but-untyped prose. Method note: compute
the oracle ground truth ONCE yourself (deterministic, not delegated),
have the fan-out reason over it for completeness + adversarial verify,
and keep R execution with the orchestrator to avoid concurrent-temp-file
races.

**Reflexes:** \[a recurring bug CLASS is best closed by one
oracle-grounded, class-complete sweep ‚Äî compute ground truth once
yourself, fan out independent lenses for completeness, adversarially
verify each finding\]\[an accuracy audit can surface a 2nd-order class
where the DATA, not the doc, is the suspect ‚Äî classify doc-bug vs
data-artifact and GATE the data-artifacts (owner‚Äôs call); fix the plain
doc-bugs\]\[the adversarial pass must veto non-claims too (reject
‚Äúfixing‚Äù prose that makes no factual assertion), not only confirm real
mismatches\]\[gate the genuine forks (scope: narrow vs full; how to
treat data-artifacts) but don‚Äôt re-gate ground-truth-determined doc
fixes ‚Äî Learning 113\]. **Apply:** any whole-artifact factual-accuracy
audit ‚Äî derive the oracle once, fan out for completeness + adversarial
verify, separate ‚Äúdoc is wrong‚Äù from ‚Äúdata looks wrong‚Äù and gate the
latter, and let the skeptic bound scope on both ends.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

#### Learning 116 ‚Äî Re-exporting an opaque / non-reproducible bundled data object = COERCE the existing object‚Äôs types in place (preserve every value) via a committed `data-raw/` script ‚Äî never re-derive from source. Investigate provenance + dependents FIRST to prune unsafe options before the scope gate, and keep the .rda + its doc + man/ + tests in ONE deliverable so you don‚Äôt reopen the doc-mismatch class. (S123, owner pick A6 ‚Äî re-export `rhesusPedigree` with corrected column types)

**What happened.** Learning 115‚Äôs flagged ‚Äúdata-artifact‚Äù (the owner‚Äôs
deferred A6) became this session‚Äôs deliverable: `rhesusPedigree` shipped
degraded types ‚Äî `id`/`sire`/`dam`/`birth` as factors, `exit` as all-NA
logical ‚Äî a `stringsAsFactors`/obfuscation-era artifact. The fix
re-exported the object with canonical types matching `examplePedigree`
(`id`/`sire`/`dam` ‚Üí character, `birth` ‚Üí `Date`, `exit` ‚Üí `Date`
all-NA), preserving **every value** (dim 375√ó8, all ids, NA patterns,
BRI2MW birth 1998-12-06, date range). Strict TDD: a pre-RED scope gate
(Type-correctness, not full canonical match ‚Äî no gratuitous `sex`
widening), a pre-RED reproducibility gate (add `data-raw/`), then RED
(31 assertions, confirmed failing) ‚Üí GREEN (coerce + doc + man regen,
all green) ‚Üí REFACTOR (N/A; flagged the adjacent redundant test
conversions). Verified: new tests pass, full suite 0/0, checkRd 0,
NAMESPACE byte-identical, 162 exports, and a real runtime smoke test.

**Coerce-in-place, never re-derive, when the generator is
non-reproducible.** A read-only provenance investigation (workflow)
established the `.rda` has NO committed generator: it was obfuscated
from `inst/extdata/rhesusPedigree_fromCenter.csv` via
[`obfuscatePed()`](https://github.com/rmsharp/nprcgenekeepr/reference/obfuscatePed.md)
and hand-saved in 2020, never scripted or seeded. Obfuscation is
non-deterministic ‚Äî re-running it from the CSV would produce DIFFERENT
obfuscated ids/dates than the shipped object (and could desync sibling
objects like `rhesusGenotypes` that share those ids). So the only sound
re-export is to load the existing object and coerce its column TYPES
while leaving VALUES untouched (`as.character`,
`as.Date(as.character(...))`, typed all-NA `Date`). The committed
`data-raw/rhesusPedigree.R` encodes exactly that constraint, is
idempotent, and establishes a reproducibility pattern other opaque
`.rda`s can follow. Lesson: ‚Äúre-export the data‚Äù does not mean
‚Äúregenerate from source‚Äù ‚Äî for an opaque/obfuscated artifact it means a
value-preserving type coercion, and the data-raw script‚Äôs job is to make
that transform reproducible, not to reinvent the original generation.

**Investigate provenance + dependents BEFORE the scope gate, so the
owner is never offered a foot-gun.** The pre-RED investigation pruned
the option set: ‚Äúre-derive from CSV‚Äù was eliminated by non-determinism,
and ‚Äúdrop the `exit` column‚Äù was eliminated because
[`getPotentialParents()`](https://github.com/rmsharp/nprcgenekeepr/reference/getPotentialParents.md)
reads `ba$exit` (dropping it breaks the object‚Äôs main consumer). Both
were plausible-sounding options that an un-investigated gate might have
offered. By verifying firsthand that no test asserts the *current*
factor/logical types (so a fix breaks nothing) and that `exit` is
required, the gate I posed contained only genuinely-safe choices.
Pruning unsafe options before asking is part of right-sizing a gate ‚Äî
don‚Äôt make the owner the safety check on options you could have ruled
out yourself.

**A data re-export and its documentation are ONE deliverable.**
S114‚ÄìS122 spent eight sessions fixing data-doc mismatches; changing a
data object‚Äôs types without updating its `R/data.R` doc + regenerating
`man/` in the SAME session would immediately reopen that exact bug class
(the doc would now describe the old degraded types). So the deliverable
bundled: `.rda` + `data-raw/` + `R/data.R` doc reversal (id
`factor`‚Üí`character`, birth/exit ‚Üí `Date`) + `man/` regen + tests +
`.Rbuildignore`. This is not scope creep ‚Äî it is the minimum coherent
unit; splitting it would ship a known inconsistency.

**Test value-preservation, not just types; smoke-test the real consumer
on the SHIPPED object.** RED pinned both the corrected types AND
preserved values (dim, NA counts, a known id‚Üídate pair, min/max range) ‚Äî
because a botched coercion can silently lose or reorder data while
satisfying type checks. For Phase 3E, ‚Äúthe suite passes‚Äù was necessary
but insufficient: the potential-parents tests do their own
`as.character`/`as.Date` conversions (now no-ops), which could mask a
type that‚Äôs still wrong. The conclusive smoke test ran
[`getPotentialParents()`](https://github.com/rmsharp/nprcgenekeepr/reference/getPotentialParents.md)
on the shipped object **directly, with no conversions** ‚Äî proving the
corrected types are usable as-shipped.

**Reflexes:** \[re-exporting an opaque/non-reproducible data object =
coerce the existing object‚Äôs TYPES in place, preserve all VALUES, via a
committed idempotent `data-raw/` script ‚Äî never re-derive from source
(non-deterministic obfuscation changes shipped values and desyncs
sibling objects)\]\[investigate provenance + dependents before the scope
gate and PRUNE unsafe options (here: re-derive-from-CSV,
drop-required-column) so the owner only chooses among safe ones\]\[a
data change and its `R/data.R` doc + `man/` regen are ONE deliverable ‚Äî
splitting them reopens the data-doc-mismatch class (S114‚ÄìS122)\]\[RED
tests for a type fix must pin VALUE preservation (dim, NA counts, known
id‚Üívalue pairs, ranges), not only types\]\[Phase 3E for a DATA change =
run the real consumer on the SHIPPED object with no test-harness
conversions ‚Äî ‚Äúsuite passes‚Äù can be masked by now-no-op conversions\].
**Apply:** any task that re-exports / corrects a bundled data object ‚Äî
check whether a reproducible generator exists (if not, coerce-in-place
via data-raw), investigate dependents to prune unsafe options before
gating, bundle the doc+man+tests with the data change, and
value-preserve in both the tests and a consumer-on-shipped-object smoke
test.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

#### Learning 117 ‚Äî When re-exporting a SIBLING opaque object on a predecessor‚Äôs one-line characterization, re-derive the FULL degradation firsthand ‚Äî the named symptom (‚Äúid still factor‚Äù) can be the least consequential of several, and a ‚Äúcosmetic type fix‚Äù can intersect downstream code semantics (factor-indexing) so scope becomes a CORRECTNESS decision, not a cosmetic one. Flag the surfaced code fragility; keep the data change atomic. (S124, owner pick A7 ‚Äî re-export `rhesusGenotypes` with corrected column types)

**What happened.** S123‚Äôs handoff flagged A7 as ‚Äúde-factor
`rhesusGenotypes$id`‚Üícharacter (id still factor) to match the
convention.‚Äù The firsthand investigation found the object ships ALL
THREE columns as factors (`id` 31 levels, `first_name` 18, `second_name`
23), and ‚Äî crucially ‚Äî that the `id` type is the one that does NOT
matter (merge coerces it either way; every consumer is id-agnostic,
confirmed independently by the dependents scan), while the two allele
columns being factors silently corrupts `addGenotype`‚Äôs output. Its
